**Letter: "A Lighthouse Keeper's Last Goodbye"**

*Written in the dim glow of a flickering oil lamp, with the wind howling like a banshee outside the stone walls of the lighthouse. The sea roars in the distance, and the sky is black as ink, heavy with impending doom.*

---

**[Letterhead: The Lighthouse at the End of the World – 1897]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

If you're reading this, then I have failed you. I have failed you in every way that matters.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of guilt that gnaws at my bones like the tide erodes the cliffs below. I’ve tried to forget, but the sea never forgets. It remembers every ship that sank, every soul that was lost, every promise that was broken. And I, too, have been lost in the dark.

Tonight, the storm comes. A storm unlike any I’ve seen in my lifetime. The sky is black, the wind is howling like a beast, and the waves are rising like the wrath of the gods. I’ve been watching the weather for days, and I know what this means. This is the storm that will take us all.

I don’t know if I’ll make it through the night. I don’t know if the light will still burn when the dawn comes. But if I do, if

**The Lighthouse at Skerry Point**  
Skerry Point, Isle of Wight  
12th October, 1893  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write to you now with a heart heavy with years of silence, and with a hand that trembles not only from the cold but from the weight of words I have long kept locked away. It has been five years since our last correspondence, and though I have often thought of you, I have never found the courage to reach out. But tonight, as the storm gathers over the sea with a fury I have not seen in my years of service, I am compelled to write. I fear that I may not live to see the dawn, and I would not have the regret of not having spoken to you one last time.  

You were always a spirited child, and I must confess that I never fully understood your yearning to leave the shores of England. I had hoped that the sea would temper your spirit, as it has tempered mine, but I see now that it was not the sea that tempered you, but your own unyielding will. You were not content to remain within the walls of a lighthouse, nor to abide by the customs of the land. You sought the world beyond, and in doing so, you left behind the only home you ever truly knew.  

I have often wondered if you still think of me, or if the years have made you forget the man who once held your

My Dearest Clara,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for I fear the night that is upon us may not bring me to morning. The wind howls like the wails of the damned, and the sea, that ever faithful companion, rages with a fury I have never known. I pray the heavens may grant me strength to see this through, and if not, may my final breath be a whisper of peace to you, my beloved daughter.

It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though I have written often, I fear the words have never reached you. I have been a fool, Clara, to let pride and fear keep me from your side. I am no better than the old sea beast that guards this lighthouse, stubborn and unyielding, refusing to yield to the tide of time. I have let my own heart grow cold, and I know now that I have been a father to you in name only, not in deed.

I have not seen you since the day you left, and though I have heard of your life in the city, I have not dared to write, fearing that my words would be too heavy for you to bear. I have heard that you are well, that you have found a husband and a home, and I have prayed that God has blessed you with joy and love. Yet, I cannot help but wonder if I have been a part of your life in any real way

**To My Dearest and Most Beloved Daughter, Eleanor,**

**I hope this missive finds you in the finest of health, and in the company of the most pleasant of company, be it your dear husband or some noble friend.** It has been a most grievous five years since our last correspondence, and I trust that you have not been the subject of any undue hardship or misfortune, though I must confess I have not heard from you since the fateful day when you chose to abandon your father’s household and depart for the grandeur of London. A most ungrateful and unfeeling act, I must say.

**Tonight, however, I find myself in a most dire predicament.** A tempest of such ferocity has been foretold, and the winds have already begun to howl like the very devils of Hell itself. I have been compelled to inspect the lighthouse, and though I have been most diligent in my duties, I must confess I have felt a most profound unease. The sea has grown restless, and the waves have risen to such a height that I fear they may well swallow the very foundations of this sacred structure. I have not the strength to endure the storm, and I have not the means to summon aid, save for the occasional passing vessel, which I have not seen for many a day.

**I am compelled to write this letter, not only to inform you of the peril that now besets me,

**Dear Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well. I’ve been meaning to write for a long time, but the words have always felt too heavy, too painful. Tonight, however, as the storm gathers strength outside and the wind howls like a wounded beast, I think I need to try again.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I’ve often wondered if you’ve ever thought of me at all. I know the silence between us has been long and deep, and I can only imagine how it must feel to you. I’ve tried to understand, to forgive, to move on—but I haven’t been able to. Not really.

You were just a child when we last saw each other, and I’ve spent every day since trying to be the father you deserved. I’ve kept the lighthouse, not just for the light it casts on the sea, but for the light it’s meant to cast on the lives of those who pass by. I’ve been alone here, watching the waves and the stars, wondering if I’ve done enough to make up for the years I missed.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The winds are rising, the sea is growing restless, and I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night. I’ve spent my life preparing for the worst, and I’ve always believed that the light of the lighthouse would guide the ships through the darkest hours. But now, as I sit here, I wonder if that

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I have been of late. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I fear the silence between us has grown heavier than the sea air on these cold winter nights.

I have not written to you because I have not known what to say. Words have always been my refuge, yet when it comes to you, they fail me. I have tried to imagine what you might think of me—of the man who once stood at the helm of this lighthouse, who once held your hand as you stood on the shore, and who now stands alone in the darkness, with only the beacon to keep me company.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves lash against the cliffs with a fury I have never known. I have spent the evening preparing the lamps, ensuring they will burn bright should I not return. I have no illusions about the danger. The sea has taken many before me, and it may take me now.

I have no regrets, not truly. I have done what I thought was right, and I have tried to be the father you deserved. But I fear I have failed you in ways I cannot undo. I have not been there when you needed me, and I have let the world shape you into someone I do not recognize.

If I do not survive this night, know that I have no bitterness in my heart.

The key points are: the lighthouse keeper hasn't spoken to his daughter in five years, there's a terrible storm coming, and he's unsure if he'll survive it. The tone needs to be that of a grizzled, older man, so maybe a bit rough around the edges but with underlying emotion.

He might mention the storm as a way to express his vulnerability. He should express his regrets, maybe talk about

**Mr. Thomas Hargrove**  
Lighthouse, Point Blackmore  
21st October, 1897  

*My Dearest Eliza,*  

I hope this letter finds you well, though I doubt it. I've been meaning to write for a long time, but I reckon you've had enough of me by now. You never answered my letters, not one. I suppose it's just as well. You've always been a troublemaker, and I've always been a fool to let you get away with it.  

I’m writing this because I reckon I might not make it through the night. The wind's howlin’ like a pack of wolves, and the sea’s up to something nasty. I’ve seen storms like this before, but this one... it’s different. I can feel it in my bones.  

You know I never wanted to be a lighthouse keeper. I wanted to be a sailor, a man with a ship and a crew, not some old man stuck on a rock with a lamp and a cat. But the sea had other plans for me, and I’ve been here for more years than I care to count.  

I’ve always told you what you needed to hear, even when you didn’t want to listen. I tried to keep you out of trouble, even when you were old enough to take care of yourself. You were a stubborn girl, and I was a stubborn man. We didn’t see eye

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, and the sea roiling as if it too mourns. It has been five years since you left, and though I have kept the light burning for every ship that passed, I have not kept it lit for you. Not truly. Not since the day you turned your back on me.

I have not spoken your name in years, not even in the silence of the night when the world is still. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the weight of the years press upon me, and I am forced to remember. I remember the last time we spoke, the way your eyes, once full of light, turned cold as the sea in winter. I remember the words you left me, sharp and unyielding, and the way they cut deeper than any blade.

I have always believed that the light of the lighthouse is a promise — to the sailors who brave the dark, to the world that relies on our vigilance. But tonight, as the wind howls and the waves crash against the stone, I wonder if I have been a fool to believe in such promises. Perhaps the light is not a promise, but a warning — that even the strongest beams cannot pierce the darkness of the heart.

I have not sought you out, not even in the quiet hours when the storm has passed and the sea lies still. I have

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write these words with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very rocks that hold this lighthouse to the earth. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if the dawn will find me still here to greet it.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. I have kept the light burning, not for the ships, but for you—though I do not know if you would recognize the man who tends it now. I have become a shadow of the father you once knew, and yet I remain here, as I always have, in this cold, lonely place.

I have not written to you, not because I do not wish to, but because I feared my words would be as hollow as the silence between the waves. I have heard the stories of your life from the sailors who come to seek shelter, and I have seen the way your name is spoken with both reverence and sorrow. You have become a legend, as distant as the stars that guide the lost.

But tonight, as the heavens weep and the sea roars, I find myself unable to hold back the truth. I have spent these years in regret, in longing, in the quiet ache of a father who could not keep his heart from breaking. I have not been worthy of your love, and I have not been

**April 12th, 1897**  
**Lighthouse, Northumberland**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the ink of a man who has spent more years than I care to count staring at the sea, and less at the sky. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the waves rise like the ghosts of those I have lost. I do not know if I will live to see the dawn. But if I do, I must say these things.

You were fifteen when you left. I remember the day you walked out the door, the way the light from the lighthouse caught the edge of your coat and made it shimmer like a promise. You said you were going to find your own way, to live your own life. I did not stop you. I did not beg you to stay. I thought, perhaps, that it would be better this way. That you would not have to see me again, not as I was.

But you did not return. Not once. Not even when the war came, and the world turned upside down. I thought you had died in some distant place, far from here, far from me. I thought I had buried you in the sea, in the sand, in the silence of the night.

And yet, here I am. Still here. Still watching the waves. Still waiting.

I do not know what has brought me to this moment. Perhaps it is the storm.

**April 12th, 1867**  
**Lighthouse, Skerryvore Head**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm that gathers on the horizon, and with hands that tremble not from the cold, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since you last set foot in this house, and five years have done little to mend the chasm between us.

I have long since ceased to expect a reply, yet I write, for I must. The sea has always been my companion, and in its fury tonight, I find myself questioning whether it is the sea or my soul that stirs with such unrest.

You were a child when you left, and though I did not say so then, I feared that you would never return. The world beyond these cliffs is a cruel and merciless place, and I have often wondered if the sins of the father are not always visited upon the son, but also upon the daughter.

I have seen the light burn through the night, and I have seen the waves crash against the rocks with a ferocity that seems almost divine in its wrath. It is a terrible thing to be alone in such a place, but it is worse to be alone in the company of silence.

I have no news of your husband, nor of your son. I have heard nothing from London, and I pray that you have not heard of me. I do not know what

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Daughter**

**Date:** April 12, 1889  
**Location:** North Sea Lighthouse, Scotland

---

**To My Dearest Elara,**

If you're reading this, then the storm has taken me. I hope you're safe. I hope you're not reading this because you've lost me.

Five years have passed since I last saw you. Five years of silence, five years of regret. I should have reached out. I should have come to you. But I was afraid. Afraid of what I might find, of what I might lose. And so I stayed here, in this cold, lonely tower, watching the sea, waiting for the world to stop moving.

I used to think that distance was a form of protection. That by keeping my head down and my mouth shut, I could keep you safe. But I was wrong. You didn't need protection. You needed me.

I remember the day you left. You were just a child, no older than ten, but you stood at the door of our home, eyes wide with fear, and said, *“Dad

**The Lighthouse at Skerry Point**  
**Skerry Point, Isle of Wight**  
**October 12th, 1883**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you from the confines of this solitary lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls. The sea, that ever-faithful companion, has grown restless this evening, and the storm that now gathers on the horizon threatens to break upon us with a fury I have not seen in many a year. I fear, with all my heart, that I may not see the dawn.

It is with a heavy soul that I take up this quill, knowing full well that the words I write may never reach you. Five years have passed since our last meeting, and the chasm between us has grown wider with each passing day. I have tried, in silence, to find the courage to reach out, but the weight of our parting has been too great to bear. Yet, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write—not for forgiveness, not for reconciliation, but for the sake of a mother who once held you close in her arms, and a father who once believed he could shield you from the world’s cruel winds.

I do not know what has transpired in your life since you left this place. I have heard from the postman a few letters of yours, but they were always addressed to a different address,

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a heavy heart, and the ink upon the page is trembling, as though it too feels the weight of the burden I must now bear. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and the days have passed like the waves upon the shore—endless, relentless, and ever-moving. I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not.

I have heard the wind howling now, and the sea is rising with a fury I have not known since the day I first took up the lantern. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take me from this world before I can see you again. I have no doubt that the waves will claim the lighthouse, and with it, my life. But I write this now, so that should I not survive, you may know the truth of what has transpired.

You must not think that I have forgotten you, my dear. I have often looked upon the sea, and in its vastness, I have seen your face. I have spoken to the wind, and in its voice, I have heard your name. I have dreamed of you, Eliza, and in those dreams, I have been with you, though you were not with me.

I have not written to you in five years, and I know not if you still remember me, or if the years have dulled the memory of your father. I have been a l

**To My Dearest and Most Precious Daughter,**

**O Lady of the Light, I Write to Thee in the Hours of the Twelfth Night, as the Skies Grow Black and the Winds Howl with a Fury Most Unnatural.**

**I hope this letter finds thee in good health and high spirits, though I fear the same cannot be said for this poor old lighthouse keeper, who, in the face of such a tempest, is but a trembling leaf upon the wind.**

**It has been five long years since our last correspondence, and I must confess, I have often pondered the cause of our estrangement. Though I do not wish to cast aspersions, I must say that I have heard whispers of thy temper and thy disdain for the ways of the old world. I fear that thou hast grown too fond of the modern ways, and perhaps hast forgotten the virtues of patience, propriety, and the sacred duty of a good daughter to her father.**

**But lo! I write to thee now not in the spirit of reproach, but with a heart full of concern. For tonight, the heavens have grown dark with a storm most fierce, and I fear that the sea, ever fickle, may rise up against us. I have seen the signs—clouds of a most ominous hue, the waves in their fury, and the wind that howls as if the very spirits of the sea have taken up residence in the tower

**Dear Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know the distance between us has grown like the sea between here and wherever you are now. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I’ve often wondered if I’ve been a good father—or if I’ve simply been too afraid to reach out.

Tonight, as I sit by the light, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury I’ve never seen. It’s as if the sea itself is trying to tell me something. I’ve always believed that the lighthouse is a place of safety, a beacon for those lost at sea. But tonight, I find myself questioning whether I’ve been the same kind of beacon for you.

You were always my greatest light. I remember the way you used to sit on my lap as a child, listening to the rhythm of the waves, your small hand in mine. You had such a quiet strength, Clara. I used to think that strength would carry you through anything. But I’ve watched you grow into a woman I don’t fully understand, and I’ve been afraid to ask questions. Afraid that the answers might reveal how much I’ve failed you.

I’ve thought about the last time we spoke. You were so angry, so hurt. I can still hear your voice, sharp and full of pain. I know I didn’t deserve that. I know I haven’t been there for you, and I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the same place you've been these past five years—cold, unfeeling, and far from home.

It has been a long, weary stretch of time since we last spoke, and I have often found myself wondering if the words I spoke that final evening were the last I would ever say to you. I have tried, in the quiet hours of the night, to write to you, to explain, to apologize, but the ink always seemed to run dry before I could form the right words. Perhaps it is best that I do not try again. Perhaps some things are best left unsaid.

The sea has been restless of late, and the wind has taken on a cruel, howling tone that reminds me of the storm that once took your mother from us. Tonight, the sky is heavy with clouds, and the waves grow ever more fierce. I have lit the beacon, as I always have, and I pray that the light will guide those who sail these waters. But I fear it may not be enough to save me.

I have not told the others at the station, but I do not believe I will make it through the night. The old bones do not hold as well as they once did, and the sea has always been a fickle mistress. I have lived with her for thirty years now, and I have come to know her moods

**Lighthouse Keeper’s Log**  
**Station: White崖 Lighthouse**  
**Date: April 12th, 1897**  
**Time: 10:47 PM**  

*To My Daughter,*

I hope this letter finds you well, though I doubt it. You’ve been gone five years now, and I’ve not heard a word from you. Not a letter, not a postcard, not even a damn whisper. I’ve tried to write, but every time I pick up a pen, I end up scrawling something about the sea or the light or the damn fog—things you’d probably rather not hear about.

I’m not writing this to apologize, nor to beg for forgiveness. I’m writing because I’m tired. Tired of the silence, tired of the cold, tired of this lighthouse and this damned storm that’s brewing out there. I’ve been keeping the light burning all night, but I don’t know how much longer I can keep it up. The wind’s howling like a banshee, and the waves are coming in like they’re trying to break the rocks themselves.

You were always too proud to come back. I know that. You thought you were better than me, better than this life, better than the sea. But I’ve seen what it does to people. I’ve seen what it does to a man who thinks he’s in control. You think you can just walk

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wail of sorrow outside the lighthouse. The sea, once a steadfast companion, now roars with fury, and I fear that tonight may be the last time I see the dawn. But if I must speak, it is to you—my daughter, my only child.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though time may heal wounds, it has not mended the silence between us. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the words you said, nor the door you closed behind you. I have carried them like a stone upon my heart, heavy and unrelenting.

You were young, and perhaps I was too rigid in my ways, too blind to the fire that burned within you. I see now that I failed to understand you, to see the girl who once looked up to me with wide, searching eyes. I was a father, but not a friend. A keeper of the light, but not of your soul.

Tonight, as the storm gathers, I find myself wondering if I will be the one to go, or if the sea will claim me before the dawn. I have watched the waves for many years, and I know their ways. They do not ask for mercy, nor do they wait. They come, they break, and they pass. So too must I face what is to come, with the grace of one who has lived

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee, the sea raging as if it would swallow the very rocks that hold this lighthouse together. The sky is black as pitch, and the waves crash with a fury that makes me fear the very heavens are in revolt. Yet, in this moment, I find myself thinking of you—my Eleanor, my only child, my heart’s sorrow.

Five long years have passed since you left this place, and I have not heard a word from you. You were a child when you went, a girl with fire in your eyes and a heart too proud to bend. I tried to reach you, to call you back, but you turned your back on me, as if I were a stranger. I do not know what you saw in that world beyond the sea that made you choose it over your mother, over me. But I have not forgotten.

I have kept the light burning for you, every night, every storm, every sorrow. I have whispered your name to the waves, hoping they would carry it to you. I have told the sea that if you ever needed a home, you would find it here, in the arms of a man who loved you more than life itself.

But now, as this storm descends upon us, I fear that my time is near. The wind is stronger than ever, and the sea grows wilder. I have seen the signs,

**April 12th, 1873**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from age, but from the weight of silence. Five years have passed since you left, and I have spent each of them staring into the sea, hoping it would speak to me. It does not. It only reflects the sky, and the sky tonight is dark as a man's soul.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with a mind like a storm and a heart like a locked door. I tried to open it. I tried to love you as you once loved me—before the world became too loud, too cold, too full of things that could not be undone. You were not the first to leave, and you will not be the last. But you were the first to take the light with you.

I have kept the lamp burning these many years. Not for the sea, but for you. I have told myself that if I should ever fail, the lamp would remain. That it would be a sign. A beacon. A promise. But tonight, the wind howls like a beast, and the sea rises like a beast, and I fear the lamp may not be enough.

**April 12, 1873**  
*St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly*  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

If you are reading these words, it may be that I am no more. The sea, that old and unrelenting adversary, has turned her wrath upon us this night, and the wind howls like a banshee through the narrow passage of this lonely tower. I write with trembling hands, for the lamp burns low and the storm grows ever louder, as though the very heavens themselves are crying out.  

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have I borne the weight of your silence. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, since the day you stood at the door and said, *“I am no longer your daughter.”* I did not answer. I could not. The words had long since been buried beneath the ash of our last farewell.  

You were always a child of the wind, I suppose—fickle, unmoored, and ever seeking a horizon beyond the reach of duty and love. I have watched you go, and in your absence, I have found myself more alone than ever. The lighthouse is a cruel companion, and the sea, that restless thing, has never been kind to me.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*Written in the dim glow of a flickering oil lamp, with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret and fear. The wind howls outside, and the sea roars like a beast. The lighthouse stands alone, as does the man who guards it.*

**To My Dearest Elise,**

**If you are reading this, I have failed you.**

I know not if the storm will claim me or if I will live to see the dawn. But if I do, I must tell you this: I have never stopped loving you. Not once. Not even when you left.

Five years. That is how long it has been since you walked out of our home, your back turned, your voice fading into the night. I tried to reach you, but you were too far gone. Too hurt. Too angry. And I—God forgive me—I was too proud to listen.

I remember the last time we spoke. You were seventeen, and I was thirty-two. You stood at the door, your eyes red, your hands shaking. You said you needed space. That you couldn’t stay. That you would return one day. But you never did.

I told myself you’d come back. That you’d understand. That you’d see the truth. But time has a cruel way of making promises brittle.

**The Lighthouse, Whitby Cliff**  
**October 12th, 1889**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for I know not whether my pen shall ever meet the page again. **To My Dearest and Most Beloved Daughter, Eleanor,**

**In the Name of the Almighty and the Most Honorable Lord of the Seas,**

**From the Remote and Perilous Heights of the Lighthouse of the North,**

**Upon the 15th Day of the Month of October, in the Year of Our Lord, 1873.**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It hath been five long and weary years since we last exchanged words, and I must confess that my heart doth ache with sorrow at the thought of thy absence. Though I have endeavoured to maintain the dignity and decorum befitting a man of my station, I must admit that the silence hath been most trying. I trust that thou art in good health and in the company of thy betrothed, Mr. Thomas Whitmore, whom I have heard is a man of commendable character, though I must say, he doth not visit as often as I would wish.

**Point Loma Lighthouse,**

**April 12th, 1897**

My dear Eliza,

I hope this finds you in better health than I am. I’ve been meaning to write for some time now, but the sea’s been restless, and I’ve had little time to think beyond the light and the storm that’s coming.

You know I’ve always been a man of few words, but I felt I should say this before it’s too late. I’ve been thinking about you, and I’ve been wondering what’s become of you. You left without a word, and I’ve not heard a single thing from you since. I tried to write, but your mother said you’d thrown all your letters away. I suppose that’s how it is. I don’t blame you, not entirely. I’ve made my share of mistakes, and I’ve not been the easiest man to live with.

But I’m not writing this to apologize, not really. I’m writing because I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night. The storm’s coming in like a beast, and the light’s been flickering. **October 12th, 1873**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

If you are reading these words, it is because I have survived the night. Or perhaps it is because you have come to see me, as I have not seen you in five years. I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast upon the cliffs, and the sea, that ever-watchful thing, roars with a fury I have never known.  

I have not written to you since the day you left, and I have not spoken of it, though I think of it often. You were but a child then, and I, a man of reason, tried to reason with you. But you were not a child, Eleanor. You were a woman, and I, a father, who could not understand the fire in your eyes. You said you would not return, that you would not be bound to this place, to this life, to me. And so you went.
